exemplar.title: A partial replication of Loftus and Palmer (1974): Investigating the effects of reconstructive memory

detailedExemplarData.title: A partial replication of Loftus and Palmer (1974): Investigating the effects of reconstructive memory

subject.title: Psychology
subject.description:
IB Psychology is a Group 3 subject in the International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP). The course examines human behavior and mental processes through scientific inquiry, exploring biological, cognitive, and sociocultural approaches to understanding behavior. Students develop critical thinking skills while investigating contemporary psychological research, research methods, and ethical considerations in psychological practice.

The course is available at Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours) and Higher Level (HL, 240 hours), with first assessment in 2027.

Assessment consists of Paper 1 (integration of concepts, content, and contexts through short-answer and extended-response questions: SL 2 hours, HL 2 hours, SL 50%, HL 40%), Paper 2 (application of psychological knowledge to research contexts: SL 1 hour, HL 2 hours, SL 25%, HL 20%), Paper 3 (HL only: research methods and data analysis, 1 hour, 20%), and Internal Assessment (research proposal development: SL 25%, HL 20%).

files.fileName: Copy of Neferane Dawelbait -  Psychology IA Loftus and Palmer FINAL VERSION (Turnitin).pdf

strengths: Aim and relevance are clearly stated and explained with realâworld legal context
Theory (reconstructive memory and schema) is accurately described and explicitly linked to the investigation
Both IV and DV are correctly stated and operationalised in the hypotheses
weaknesses: Minor reliance on textbook phrasing prevents a fully original description
Null hypothesis lacks specification of statistical criterion (e.g. pâ¤0.05)
DV scale could be further operationalised (e.g. specifying increments for speed estimates)
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The introduction consistently meets the top band descriptors: the aim and its relevance are well explained, theory is described and connected, and hypotheses are operationalised. Minor issues in phrasing and extra detail prevent a perfect 6, so a score of 5 accurately reflects a strong but not flawless performance.

strengths: Independent-measures design is well justified to avoid order and demand effects
Controlled variables and choice of materials are explained in relation to confound reduction
weaknesses: Volunteer sampling explanation lacks depth regarding external validity bias
Rationale for the 30 sec time limit is not provided, weakening construct validity
Manipulation check omission reduces confidence that participants noticed the critical verb
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: While the design and materials sections are clearly explained (band 4), the sampling discussion is superficial (band 3) and key justifications (time limit, manipulation check) are missing. Overall performance sits in the mid-band, warranting a score of 3.

strengths: Appropriate use of median and Mann-Whitney test demonstrates correct choice of statistics
Interpretation of findings is thorough and linked to hypothesis direction
weaknesses: Inferential conclusion is based on a misinterpretation of Uâvalue significance
Medians are misquoted in the narrative, causing confusion
Graphical presentation lacks error bars and clear axis labels
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Statistical choices are appropriate, and interpretation is robust and hypothesisâlinked (band 5), but errors in inferential logic, inconsistent reporting of medians, and flawed graphing reduce accuracy to the 3-4 band. A score of 4 reflects competent but error-laden analysis.

strengths: Findings are discussed in depth with explicit reference to schema theory
Strengths and limitations are explained and tied to validity/reliability
Modifications are explicitly linked to identified limitations and well justified
weaknesses: Discussion of alternative explanations is somewhat brief
Feasibility challenges for the matchedâpairs design are not addressed
Some suggested modifications do not fully consider control of cognitive factors
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The evaluation demonstrates a clear, theory-linked discussion, relevant strengths/limitations analysis, and justified modifications, matching the top band. Slight brevity and missing feasibility considerations prevent a perfect 6, so a score of 5 is appropriate.

strandSelections.reasoning: The bar chart correctly depicts median speeds and addresses the hypothesis, but lacks error bars or clear axis units and shows minimal labelling detail. These presentation flaws count as âerrorsâ, so the strand sits in the 3-4 band; a mid-band 3 reflects adequate but imperfect execution.

The student links statistical outcome to rejecting the null and explicitly relates results to the direction predicted, while also discussing overlap and reliability. Interpretation is thorough and data-focused, meeting the 5-6 descriptor; occasional numerical inconsistencies prevent a 6, so a 5 is awarded.

Both null and alternative hypotheses are given; the IV (verb: contacted vs smashed) and DV (estimated speed 20â100 km hâ»Â¹) are **clearly stated and operationalised**. The wording shows the student understands how each variable will be measured, so the top band is justified (score 5 to reflect solid but not exceptional sophistication).

Volunteer sampling, participant characteristics and brief justification (time constraints, English proficiency) are given; the student notes convenience and some limitations, which constitutes an **explanation**, though it is fairly brief and focuses on practicality more than methodological rigour, so a low 3-4 band mark (3) is most fitting.

Reconstructive memory and schema theory are **accurately described** and the student **explains how these frameworks account for the predicted verb effect**, repeatedly connecting theory to the planned manipulation. Minor textbook phrasing prevents a perfect 6, but the depth and clarity meet the 5-6 descriptor.

Findings are **discussed in depth** with reference to schema theory, elaborating on how verb intensity activates different schemas. The discussion moves beyond simple description, satisfying the top band; slight brevity on alternative explanations keeps the mark to 5 rather than 6.

**Aim and relevance** are explicitly stated (to examine the effect of the critical verb on speed estimates) and the student **explains** why this matters for understanding eyewitness reliability and the malleability of memory. The link to real-world legal contexts shows clear relevance rather than a mere identification, comfortably fitting the top band; a mid-band 5 is appropriate because the explanation, while clear, is concise rather than comprehensive.

Multiple strengths and limitations for design, sample and procedure are **explained** and each is explicitly tied to validity or reliability, aligning with the 5-6 descriptor. The coverage is clear but not exhaustive, so a 5 is most appropriate.

Key controlled variables (location, time, standardised video/instructions) and materials are listed **with reasons** for each, showing clear understanding of how they reduce confounds. This fulfils the âexplainedâ criterion; breadth and explicit rationale justify the upper score of 4 within the 3-4 band.

Proposed improvements (matched-pairs, stratified sampling, more realistic stimuli, stricter timing) are **explicitly linked** to the stated limitations and justified in terms of enhancing validity and reliability. This meets the highest descriptor; the justification is solid though not deeply technical, so a score of 5 is awarded.

The independent-measures design is **explained**, with rationale linked to avoiding order effects and demand characteristics. The explanation goes beyond description, satisfying the 3-4 band; the clarity and relevance merit the top of that band (4).

Descriptive (median, SD, IQR) and inferential (Mann-Whitney) statistics are applied appropriately. Minor inaccuraciesâmis-stating that U > critical indicates significance and inconsistent median valuesâconstitute errors, placing the work in the 3-4 band. Given the otherwise correct selection and use of statistics, a 4 is warranted.

annotations.comment: The alternative hypothesis operationalises IV ("contacted" vs. "smashed") and DV (speed estimate) clearly, showing strong variable control.
The background paragraph provides a clear definition of memory with a credible citation, establishing solid theoretical context.
Reconstructive memory and schema theory are well articulated, showing how schemas influence recall and supporting the studyâs theoretical foundation.
Consider ending the literature review with a clear statement linking this background directly to the specific aim of your current replication.
The aim is stated clearly and its relevance to eyewitness reliability and legal contexts is explicitly explained.
Consider adding a manipulation check question post-task to confirm participants noticed the specific critical word.
Table 1 presents medians accurately, but the narrative misquotes them (should be 32.5 and 55), which causes confusion.
The discussion of overlapping standard deviations is unclear; clarify how overlap informs the reliability of differences.
The bar chart lacks error bars and clearly labeled axes, which reduces the readerâs ability to assess variability.

The null hypothesis is stated but could be strengthened by specifying the statistical criterion (e.g.
$$p\le0.05$$) or by clarifying directionality.

Could you operationalise the DV scale further, for instance by specifying how estimates will be recorded (e.g.
in 5mph increments)?
The independent measures design is well justified to avoid order and practice effects, demonstrating clear design reasoning.
Volunteer sampling is described but the discussion of its impact on external validity is superficial; expand on potential bias.
Justify the choice of video length and playback speed explicitly to strengthen construct validity of your materials.
Controlling the testing location to minimize distractions is well explained and supports high internal validity.
The scripted instructions reduce interpersonal variability effectively, ensuring consistency across participants.
The 30sec time limit is described but lacks rationale; explain why this duration best balances recall time and consistency.
Link the inferential outcome directly back to the alternative hypothesis statement to improve coherence.
The discussion of findings integrates schema theory effectively, illustrating how verb intensity triggers different recall schemas.
Appropriate use of the median for skewed data demonstrates a solid grasp of descriptive statistic selection.
The MannâWhitney interpretation is incorrect: significance occurs when $$U\le U_{crit}$$, not when $$U>U_{crit}$$.
Explicitly justify choosing a one-tailed test and link this decision to your directional hypothesis for clarity.

Reference formatting is inconsistent (e.g.
italics and hanging indents); apply a consistent style (APA/MLA) for academic rigor.

Ensure URLs in the reference list follow citation guidelines (e.g.
link format, retrieval dates) to improve clarity and consistency.
The matchedâpairs design suggestion is apt but could benefit from discussion of feasibility and potential participant matching challenges.

Quantify how volunteer sampling might bias results (e.g.
by underrepresenting certain groups) to deepen your evaluation.
Modification proposals are well justified and directly linked to limitations, demonstrating strong evaluative reasoning.
Procedure evaluation notes cognitive factors but does not link specific modifications to controlling stress or timing in future studies.
The conclusion succinctly ties results back to Loftus and Palmer (1974), reinforcing the studyâs contribution to memory research.
